title: Writing - Part 2
description: Choose one of the following writing tasks and write your answer in 140-190 words.

tasks.title: Article
tasks.prompt:
You see this notice in an English-language magazine:

'Work experience: valuable stepping stone or a waste of time? Write an article sharing your views on work experience placements, describing your own experience if you have had one, and advising other young people on how to make the most of them.'

Write your article.
tasks.modelAnswer:
The Real Workplace: Why Work Experience Is Worth It

I still remember the first day of my two-week placement at a local architecture firm. I arrived terrified, convinced I knew nothing and would be asked to make tea for a fortnight. What actually happened transformed my understanding of my future career — and of myself.

Work experience gives young people something no classroom can replicate: the reality of professional life. You discover what a career actually feels like from the inside, which is often very different from how it appears from the outside. I learned more about architecture in those two weeks than in an entire semester of studying.

To make the most of any placement, I would strongly advise being proactive. Ask questions, volunteer for tasks, and treat every interaction as an opportunity to learn. Do not just observe — participate. And always follow up with a thank-you email, as professional networks begin with small gestures.

Work experience may feel daunting, but the insights and contacts you gain are genuinely invaluable. Approach it with curiosity and enthusiasm, and it will reward you.

tasks.title: Email
tasks.prompt:
Your English friend Alex has just graduated and is looking for their first job. Write an email to Alex giving advice on how to write a strong CV, how to prepare for job interviews, and how to stay motivated during a long job search.

Write your email to Alex.
tasks.modelAnswer:
Hi Alex,

Congratulations on graduating — that is a huge achievement! Job searching can feel overwhelming at first, but with the right approach, you will find something great. Here is my honest advice.

For your CV, keep it clear, concise, and no longer than two pages. Tailor it specifically to each job you apply for, using keywords from the job description. Quantify your achievements wherever possible — for example, saying you 'managed a team of five' is much stronger than simply saying you 'had leadership experience'.

For interviews, preparation is everything. Research the company thoroughly, prepare answers to common questions using the STAR method (Situation, Task, Action, Result), and always have two or three thoughtful questions ready to ask them at the end.

As for staying motivated during a long search — set yourself daily goals, celebrate small wins, and try not to take rejections personally. Every interview, even an unsuccessful one, makes you better at the next.

You've got this, I am absolutely sure of it!

Send me your CV if you'd like a second pair of eyes on it!

Best,
Nick

tasks.title: Report
tasks.prompt:
Your college careers department has asked you to write a report on students' awareness of career options and the support available to help them plan their futures. Include findings about what students currently know, what help they feel they need, and recommendations for improving careers guidance at the college.

Write your report.
tasks.modelAnswer:
Report on Careers Guidance Provision at Ashford College

Introduction
This report presents findings from a survey of 190 students regarding their awareness of career options and satisfaction with the careers support currently provided by the college.

Current Awareness and Knowledge
The survey revealed significant gaps in students' career awareness. Only 34% felt confident about the career path they wished to pursue, and over half reported feeling overwhelmed by the range of options available. Many students had limited knowledge of apprenticeships and vocational routes, focusing almost exclusively on traditional university pathways.

Support Students Feel They Need
Respondents expressed a strong desire for one-to-one careers counselling sessions, visits from professionals across a variety of industries, and more detailed guidance on how to search for jobs and write effective CVs. Several students requested information specifically about careers in creative and digital industries, which they felt were underrepresented in current provision.

Recommendations
The careers department should increase the frequency of individual guidance appointments, establish a programme of guest speakers from diverse sectors, and introduce a compulsory careers module for all first-year students. An updated online resources hub would also significantly improve accessibility of information.
